question: Explain the difference between write-through and write-back cache policies. Which one provides better performance and why?
expectedPoints: Write-through writes to both cache and main memory simultaneously
Write-back writes only to cache initially, writes to main memory on eviction
Write-back provides better performance due to reduced main memory writes
Write-through ensures data consistency without additional complexity

question: Define temporal and spatial locality. How do these principles improve cache performance?
expectedPoints: Temporal locality: recently accessed items likely accessed again
Spatial locality: nearby items likely accessed soon
Cache exploits locality by keeping recently accessed data and prefetching adjacent data
This reduces average memory access time by increasing hit rate

question: Compare direct mapping, fully associative mapping, and set-associative mapping in terms of hit rate and hardware complexity.
expectedPoints: Direct: lowest hit rate, simplest hardware (one comparator)
Fully associative: highest hit rate, most complex (N comparators for N lines)
Set-associative: moderate hit rate, moderate complexity (set of comparators)
Trade-off between cost and performance

question: What are the three types of cache misses? Explain each briefly.
expectedPoints: Compulsory/Cold miss: first access to a block
Conflict miss: multiple blocks mapping to same location (direct/set-associative)
Capacity miss: cache too small to hold all needed blocks
Each requires different optimization strategies

question: Explain the working of LRU replacement policy in cache memory.
expectedPoints: LRU tracks the order of access to blocks in a set
On replacement, evicts the block not accessed for longest time
Requires hardware to maintain access history (counters/stack)
Provides good approximation of optimal replacement

question: Describe the structure of a cache address with tag, index, and offset fields.
expectedPoints: Offset: identifies byte within cache line (block)
Index: selects the cache set/line for direct/set-associative mapping
Tag: identifies the memory block stored in the cache line
Combined to uniquely identify data in main memory

question: What is the 'memory wall' problem and how does cache memory address it?
expectedPoints: Memory wall: gap between CPU speed and memory access time widening over years
CPU clock rates improved faster than memory latency
Cache acts as high-speed buffer between CPU and slow main memory
Reduces effective memory access time by keeping frequently used data close to CPU

question: A computer system has a direct-mapped cache with 16 KB size, 64-byte block size, and 32-bit address. Calculate: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Number of sets, (iii) Tag bits, (iv) Index bits, (v) Offset bits.
expectedPoints: Cache size = 16 KB = 16384 bytes
Block size = 64 bytes = 2^6, so offset bits = 6
Number of lines = 16384/64 = 256 lines
For direct-mapped: lines = sets, so index bits = log2(256) = 8
Tag bits = 32 - 8 - 6 = 18 bits
Demonstrates cache address decomposition

question: Explain the concept of cache coherency in multi-processor systems. What is the difference between write-invalidate and write-update protocols?
expectedPoints: Cache coherency ensures all processors see same data for same memory location
Write-invalidate: on write, invalidate other copies, writer updates later
Write-update: on write, immediately update all other copies
Write-invalidate typically better (reduces bus traffic)
MESI protocol is common implementation

question: Design a 2-way set-associative cache with 256 sets, 64-byte block size, and 32-bit address. Determine: (i) Number of cache lines, (ii) Tag bits, (iii) Index bits, (iv) Offset bits, (v) Total tag array size in bits.
expectedPoints: 256 sets × 2 ways = 512 cache lines
Offset bits = log2(64) = 6 bits
Index bits = log2(256) = 8 bits
Tag bits = 32 - 8 - 6 = 18 bits
Tag array size = 512 × 18 = 9216 bits or 1.125 KB
Shows cache organization calculations

question: Compare L1, L2, and L3 caches in terms of size, speed, and proximity to CPU. Why is a multi-level cache hierarchy used?
expectedPoints: L1: smallest (16-64KB), fastest (<1ns), directly attached to CPU core
L2: larger (256KB-1MB), slower (3-10ns), may be shared
L3: largest (4-16MB), slowest (10-20ns), shared across cores
Multi-level provides trade-off between speed, size, and cost
Earlier levels use faster technology, later levels use larger, cheaper technology
Inclusion property ensures data consistency
